one_line_takeaway: A taxonomy-building survey that unifies jailbreak attacks and defenses across text/image/audio/video multimodal generative models under a four-level (input/encoder/generator/output) lifecycle, cataloging methods, datasets, and metrics while flagging video, audio, and any-to-any combined outputs as the biggest open gaps.
key_finding: The survey's main analytical takeaways: (1) prior jailbreak surveys ([22]-[25]) are siloed by single output modality (any-to-text via LLM backbones OR any-to-image via diffusion backbones) and lack a framework spanning text/image/audio/video and different generative structures -- this gap motivates the unified four-level lifecycle. (2) Video and audio modalities are markedly underexplored relative to text and image, because temporal dependencies (video) and acoustic features (audio) pose distinct attack/defense challenges. (3) Harmful COMBINED multimodal outputs from any-to-any models are largely unstudied even though such combined text-image-video-audio outputs can amplify credibility and psychological impact. (4) Joint cross-modal optimization of attacks (e.g., simultaneously optimizing text and image prompts) is an emerging, more deceptive attack direction. (5) Low-perplexity adversarial prompts are increasingly important to attackers because high-perplexity gibberish is easily filtered.
core_contribution: This is a survey (not an empirical paper). Its own contribution is a unified organizing framework and taxonomy for jailbreak attacks and defenses against MULTIMODAL generative models, which the authors claim is the first to span text, image, audio, and video across three model families: Any-to-Text (LLM-backbone, e.g. IT2T/VT2T/AT2T), Any-to-Vision (diffusion-backbone, e.g. T2I/T2V), and Any-to-Any (unified-backbone) models. The central abstraction is a 'generalized lifecycle' that decomposes any multimodal jailbreak into four discrete levels of interaction with the model -- (1) Input level, (2) Encoder level, (3) Generator level, and (4) Output level -- and maps both attack techniques and corresponding defenses onto each level. On top of this, the authors provide a categorization of attack methods (their Table III), defense mechanisms (Table IV), and evaluation datasets/metrics (Tables V and VI), plus a discussion of open challenges and future directions. The framework itself is the authors' synthesis; the individual attacks/defenses it catalogs (AutoDAN, Jailbreak-in-Pieces, MMA-diffusion, FigStep, HADES, etc.) are cited third-party work, not this paper's own methods.
threat_model: As a survey, the paper does not propose a new threat model but organizes the field's existing ones. The organizing adversary is a jailbreak attacker seeking to bypass a multimodal model's built-in safety/alignment mechanisms and induce harmful content (harmful text, images, video, or audio). Attacker capability is graded by access level along the four-stage lifecycle: black-box attackers operate only on inputs (crafting adversarial prompts, typographic images, cross-modal payloads); stronger adversaries gain access to the encoder (optimizing adversarial inputs to inject malicious information into the latent space) or the full generator (leveraging activations, gradients, and fine-tuning to raise attack effectiveness); output-level actors iteratively refine inputs based on observed outputs. Defenders are modeled symmetrically at each of the same four levels (protective input cues, latent-space filtering, robustness training/alignment, and output post-processing/toxicity detection). The novel exposure the survey emphasizes is that combining modalities creates new cross-modal interaction spaces that adversaries can exploit to evade unimodal safeguards.
limitations: Inherent to a survey: it contributes taxonomy and synthesis rather than new measurements, so it provides no head-to-head empirical comparison of attack/defense effectiveness under a common benchmark -- ASR values are not aggregated across methods, leaving relative strength of catalogued techniques unquantified in the paper itself. Coverage is a snapshot (arXiv v2, Dec 2024) and will date quickly given the fast-moving field. The four-level lifecycle, while unifying, is a coarse abstraction that can flatten important architecture-specific distinctions. The paper acknowledges that its own future-work section identifies large unaddressed areas (video/audio, any-to-any combined outputs), i.e., the surveyed literature is itself thin in those regions. As a review, it does not validate the correctness or reproducibility of the claims made by the papers it cites.

datasets_used: Note: these are datasets CATALOGED in the survey's Table V, not run by the authors. AdvBench (I+T->T)
quantitative_results: Table V dataset volumes (catalogued, not measured by authors): JailBreakV-28K = 28,000 samples across 16 themes
HADES = 750 (GPT-4 generates 50 keywords per harmful category, 3 instructions per keyword, 5 categories)
HarmBench = 510 (4 themes); AdvBench = 500; FigStep = 500 (10 scenarios x 50 GPT-4-generated questions, 10 themes)

Metric definitions given (not values): Attack Success Rate ASR = N_success / N_total; Prompt Perplexity PPL; Frechet Inception Distance FID (lower = better image quality); CLIP Score = cosine similarity between adversarial image and clean-prompt embeddings
baselines_compared: A survey with no experiments, so no empirical baselines. It positions itself AGAINST prior jailbreak surveys [22]-[25], which the authors characterize as limited to a single output modality -- any-to-text [22],[23],[26] OR any-to-image [24],[25] -- and lacking a unified multi-modality, multi-architecture framework.

Robustness metric used across catalogued attacks: Attack Success Rate (ASR). Utility metrics catalogued: Prompt Perplexity (PPL), FID, CLIP Score. Toxicity detectors catalogued in Table VI (e.g., Stable Diffusion safety checker, fine-tuned RoBERTa-based classifiers) used as automatic evaluators.
